coredata.dc:title: Supported carbon dots serve as high-performance adsorbent for the retention of trace cadmium
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yi-Kun
Yang, Ting
Chen, Ming-Li
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal route, and a new solid-phase extraction (SPE) adsorbent was developed by immobilizing the carbon dots on the microcarrier cytopore, shortly termed as C-dots@cytopore. The C-dots@cytopore composites were characterized by means of FT-IR, SEM, XPS and fluorescence spectrometry. The performance of the composites for the adsorption of heavy metals was thoroughly evaluated by using cadmium as a model. The binding of cadmium on C-dots@cytopore fits Langmuir adsorption, and the adsorption dynamic follows pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics model. The binding of cadmium was pH-dependent, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 2420μgg−1 obtained at pH 4–7. A novel separation and preconcentration procedure was thus developed for trace cadmium using the C-dots@cytopore composites as SPE sorbent. The retained cadmium could be readily eluted and recovered by a 0.1molL−1 HNO3 solution and further quantified with graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS). With a sample volume of 1.0mL, an enrichment factor of 17.85 was obtained with a detection limit of 1.8ngL−1 and a RSD value of 2.6% at 0.1μgL−1 (n = 9). The procedure was further validated by analyzing cadmium in certified reference materials and a series of environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-phase extraction
Preconcentration
Cadmium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The recovery of cadmium from C-dots@cytopore
Analytical performance and validation of the method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Images of the C-dots solution under daylight (A) and UV-light (at a wavelength of 228nm) (B).
Images of cytopore (A) and C-dots@cytopore (B) under fluorescence microscope. The inset photograph shows the image of cytopore and C-dots@cytopore, respectively.
SEM images of cytopore (A-B) and C-dots@cytopore (C-D). (A) cytopore (20,000×), (B) cytopore (50,000×), (C) C-dots@cytopore (20,000×), (D) C-dots@cytopore (50,000×).
FT-IR spectra of carbon dots and C-dots@cytopore. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The pH dependence of adsorption efficiency of Cd2+ by C-dots@cytopore (black square dot) and cytopore (red round dot). Sample: 1.0mL (2μgL−1 Cd2+); Adsorbent: 1.0mg. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A)The dependence of elution efficiency of Cd2+ on the eluent concentration; (B) The dependence of elution efficiency of Cd2+ (black square dot) and enrichment factor on the eluent volume (blue round dot). Conditions in A: sample volume: 1000μL, 0.2µgL−1 Cd2+; pH 6.0; eluent volume: 50μL; Conditions in B: sample volume: 1000μL, 0.2µgL−1 Cd2+; pH 6.0; eluent: 0.1molL−1 HNO3.

The dependence of adsorption and recovery efficiency of Cd2+ on the ionic strength. Conditions in A: sample volume: 1000μL, 2.0µgL−1 Cd2+; pH 6.0; Conditions in B: sample volume: 1000μL, 0.2µgL−1 Cd2+; pH 6.0; eluent volume: 50μL.
The effect of co-existing ions on the recovery of the retained cadmium.
Characteristic performance data for the selective adsorption of Cd2+ by C-dots@cytopore with detection by GFAAS.
Comparison of the analytical performance for the detection of Cd(II) obtained by the developed method and the other similar methods.
Determination of cadmium in a certified reference material and surface water samples (n = 3, Mean ± S.D.).

head.ce:title: Supported carbon dots serve as high-performance adsorbent for the retention of trace cadmium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal route, and a new solid-phase extraction (SPE) adsorbent was developed by immobilizing the carbon dots on the microcarrier cytopore, shortly termed as C-dots@cytopore. The C-dots@cytopore composites were characterized by means of FT-IR, SEM, XPS and fluorescence spectrometry. The performance of the composites for the adsorption of heavy metals was thoroughly evaluated by using cadmium as a model. The binding of cadmium on C-dots@cytopore fits Langmuir adsorption, and the adsorption dynamic follows pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics model. The binding of cadmium was pH-dependent, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 2420μgg−1 obtained at pH 4–7. A novel separation and preconcentration procedure was thus developed for trace cadmium using the C-dots@cytopore composites as SPE sorbent. The retained cadmium could be readily eluted and recovered by a 0.1molL−1 HNO3 solution and further quantified with graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS). With a sample volume of 1.0mL, an enrichment factor of 17.85 was obtained with a detection limit of 1.8ngL−1 and a RSD value of 2.6% at 0.1μgL−1 (n = 9). The procedure was further validated by analyzing cadmium in certified reference materials and a series of environmental water samples.

The unique solid-phase extraction (SPE) adsorbent C-dots@Cytopore composites were prepared by immobilizing the carbon dots on the microcarrier Cytopore for the separation and preconcentration of trace cadmium.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium is a highly toxic metal element which gives rise to serious environmental pollution problems [1]. With the rapid development of industry, large amount of industrial wastes were discharged into the environment without treatment, causing excessive cadmium accumulation in the natural world. Cadmium can easily enter into the human body through food chain but it is generally very hard to be metabolized out of the system [2,3]. Even at very low concentration levels, it could cause serious damages to human organs, e.g., anemia, abdominal pain, kidney and skeletal systems, in addition to its carcinogenic feature [4–6]. Thus the accurate quantification of cadmium and the timely warning of its risk, especially at ultra-trace level in environmental samples, are of significant importance.

Due to the high complexity of the sample matrixes and the low level of cadmium in real samples, an effective and efficient separation-preconcentration step prior to its analysis is generally highly desired. At the present, common sample pretreatment approaches including cloud point extraction [7], liquid-liquid microextraction [8], liquid-liquid extraction [9] and solid-phase extraction (SPE) [10] have been extensively applied in the separation and preconcentration of various trace metal elements. Compared with other preconcentration processes, SPE technique is most widely employed on account of the high extraction efficiency, less solvent consumption and relatively convenient operation [11,12]. Moreover, diverse choices of solid-phase adsorbents and ease of surface modification of the materials provided broad spaces for the development of SPE of heavy metals.

A variety of novel materials, e.g., carbonaceous material [13], molecular or ion-imprinted polymers [14,15], mesoporous material [16] and magnetic material [17], have been widely used in solid phase extraction. Among these, carbon nanostructures attract extensive attention relied on their unique physical and chemical properties [18]. At this respect, nanomaterials could be classified as zero-, one-, two- and three-dimensional nanostructures. Nowadays, a large amount of carbon nanophase materials including nanotubes, nanosheets and nanofibers have been successfully applied in the separation and enrichment of the analytes at ultra-trace level [19–21]. Carbon dots is a type of zero-dimensional nano-sized material [22]. Massive oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon dots, e.g., carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, offer sufficient binding sites for multiple metal cations through electrostatic attraction, complexation and other interactions. It is remarkable that different approaches were exploited to regulate the functional groups contained on the carbon dots in order to improve the selectivity towards specific metals [23,24]. Carbon dots with large surface area provide more active sites with respect to that provided by bulky adsorbent, facilitating fast adsorption and desorption dynamics. Furthermore, benefited from abundant material sources, convenient and diverse fabrication process and stable physical/chemical properties, carbon dots are qualified as a good alternative for the SPE of metal cations [25,26]. However, it is known that in practice carbon dots are very hard to be separated from the aqueous medium through centrifugation due to their tiny size (in the range of approximately 1–10nm) and hydrophilic property, which largely limits their application in the field of sample pretreatment.

In the present study, carbon dots were employed as a novel adsorbent for the SPE of trace level of heavy metals. The carbon dots were prepared through a one-pot hydrothermal method and then immobilized onto the surface of the microcarrier cytopore, resulting in a unique solid adsorbent, i.e., C-dots@cytopore composites. The supported carbon dots which merged the mechanical strength together with the micro-size property were proven to be very suitable for the adsorption of cadmium. The effects of various analytical parameters related to the SPE of cadmium, i.e., the acidity of sample solution, the volume and concentration of the eluent as well as interfering effects were studied. The novel composites were successfully applied in the separation and preconcentration of trace level of cadmium in real environmental samples.

GGX-200atomic absorption spectrometer (Beijing Hai-Guang Instrument Co. Ltd, Beijing, China) with deuterium background correction was employed. Cadmium hollow cathode lamp (General Research Institute for Non-ferrous Metals, Beijing, China) was used as light source at a wavelength of 228.8nm and operated at 5mA, with a 0.4nm slit width. Pyrolytically coated graphite tubes were used for quantification. GFAAS temperature program for the detection of cadmium was used as in the following (ramp/holding time): drying at 100°C (15/10s), pyrolysis at 350°C (15/10s), atomization at 1900°C (1/3s), and cleaning at 2100°C (1/3s).

All the reagents used were at least of analytical reagent grade. A stock solution of cadmium (1000mgL−1) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of cadmium pellets in 1.0molL−1 HNO3. Working standards of cadmium at various concentrations were obtained by step-wise dilution of the stock solution. pH value of the sample solution was adjusted by using diluted HNO3 (1.0molL−1) or NaOH (2.0molL−1). Deionized (DI) water (18MΩcm−1) was used throughout.

The microcarrier beads cytopore (GE healthcare, USA) are positively charged with a diameter ranging from 200 to 280µm. Other chemicals used including ethylene diamine tetracetic acid [C10H14N2Na2O8·2H2O], nitric acid, sodium hydroxide, sodium nitrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China).
The preparation of C-dots@cytopore composites involves two-steps: carbon dots preparation and their ensuing immobilization.

The carbon dots used in the present study were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal route [27,28]. Shortly, 1.0g of EDTA-Na2 was dissolved in 10mL of DI water with the assistance of ultrasonic treatment. The solution was then transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 12h. After the reaction was cooled down to ambient temperature, the obtained carbon dots solution was filtered through a 0.22µm membrane to remove the excessive insoluble impurity. The finally obtained solution was pale yellow and transparent. As shown in Fig. 1, blue-green fluorescence emission was observed under UV radiation at 228nm.

The immobilization of carbon dots was accomplished via electrostatic interaction between negatively charged groups on the surface of carbon dots and positively charged cytopore surface. 10mL of carbon dots solution was added into 5mL of cytopore microcarrier beads suspension (10mgmL−1, rinsed with DI water) and standard for overnight under stirring (250rpm, 37°C). The carbon dots loaded microcarriers were rinsed with DI water for 3 times and re-suspended in 5mL of DI water to give a final concentration of 10mgmL−1 for future use.

C-dots@cytopore composites were characterized by FT-IR, XPS and SEM. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) from 500cm−1 to 4000cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) curves were performed with a ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Scanning electron microscopic images (SEM) were taken on a S-3400 scanning electronic microscope (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) to observe the surface of the C-dots@cytopore composites. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan).

1mL of sample solution or standard solution at pH 6.0 was mixed with 1.0mg of the C-dots@cytopore composites followed by vigorous shaking on the vortex mixer for 2min for cadmium adsorption. Cadmium was retained onto the adsorbent surface rapidly during this process. After two minutes static settlement, the supernatant is discarded and 50μL of nitric acid (0.1molL−1) was added as eluent under vigorous shaking for 10min. The eluate was completely separated from the composites under the action of gravity and the concentration of Cd2+ in the eluate was determined by GFAAS.

Spring water was collected from Da-Qing-Gou valley natural protective region (Inner Mongolia Province, China) and snow water was collected from the campus of Northeastern University. Both water samples were filtered through a 0.22µm membrane filter and adjusted to pH 6.0 before use. Certified reference material GBW(E)080039 (Second Institute of Oceanography of the State Oceanic Administration, China) was used for procedure validation and adjusted to pH 6.0 before subject to the adsorption process.

The obtained carbon dots are superiorly soluble and easy to disperse in aqueous solution, with negative charge on the surface in a neutral medium. Through electrostatic interaction [29], carbon dots can be assembled on the surface of cytopore spheres, composing of porous cellulose functionalized with positively charged diethyl aminoethyl groups (DEAE). The decoration could be easily observed by naked eye under inverted fluorescence microscope, as shown in Fig. 2A-B. It could be seen that after decoration with carbon dots, strong green fluorescence emission from C-dots@cytopore was observed and the C-dots@cytopore composites showed excellent fluorescent property. In order to further confirm the successful immobilization of carbon dots on cytopore spheres, the C-dots@cytopore composites were stripped by vigorous ultrasonic vibration in an alkali medium, and the fluorescence property of the stripped carbon dots was evaluated, and the results were shown in Fig. S1. It could be seen that after stripping from the surface of cytopore beads, the carbon dots remain their fluorescence property, with the maximum emission wavelength unchanged at 400nm when excited at 270nm.

SEM images of C-dots@cytopore and bare cytopore beads were illustrated in Fig. 3. It is obvious that bare cytopore showed smooth and uniform surface (Fig. 3A-B), while after decoration with carbon dots, rough surface was observed, and clearly adhesive particles were observed on the surface of cytopore (Fig. 3C-D), which is most probably attributed to the immobilized carbon dots.

FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots in Fig. 4 (red line) showed the characteristic absorptions of carboxyl at 1400cm−1 and 1640cm−1, indicating that the carbon dots were obtained by carboxylation. The structure of COO- was a multi-electron conjugated system, and the absorptions consist of two C˭O vibration bands, i.e., the antisymmetric stretching vibration at 1640cm−1 and the symmetric stretching vibration at 1400cm−1. The absorption at 3430cm−1 was attributed to the hydroxy from chemically bonded water by carbon dots. As for the C-dots@cytopore, the new absorptions at 2900cm−1 and 1060cm−1 were assigned to -CH2 and -C-O stretching originated from the hydrocarbon and oxygen containing functional groups of cytopore. The characteristic absorption bands at 1400cm−1 and 1640cm−1 were consistent with those observed in free carbon dots, indicating the presence of carbon dots on the surface of cytopore.

X-ray photoelectron spectra illustrated in Fig. S2 revealed the elemental composition of bare cytopore and C-dots@cytopore, respectively. To provide more accurate chemical environmental information, narrow scan was performed for certain elemental core levels to obtain high-resolution spectra. As shown in Fig. S2, the signal of peak maxima located at (286.5 ± 0.2) eV and (532.5 ± 0.2) eV binding energy (belongs to C-O) were enhanced with respect to that of the bare cytopore, while a decrease was observed for that at (284.6 ± 0.2) eV (belongs to C–C, C–H). These results showed a higher O content after the decoration because of the abundant COO- on the surface of carbon dots. Table S1 summarized the quantitative analytical results of the percentage of element composite derived from XPS analysis.

The acidity of the adsorption medium is an important parameter in the process of heavy metal adsorption. It exhibits a pronounced influence on both the existing forms of target metal species and the surface charge of the adsorbent [30,31]. Thus, the effects of pH value on the adsorption of Cd2+ by C-dots@cytopore composites and bare cytopore were thoroughly investigated within a range of pH 1–12. As illustrated in Fig. 5, a maximum adsorption by C-dots@cytopore was observed at pH 4–7, corresponding to an adsorption efficiency of >95%. Meanwhile, however, the adsorption of Cd2+ by bare cytopore was virtually not observed at the same experimental conditions. On the other hand, the adsorption of cadmium by the C-dots@cytopore decreased rapidly in either strong acidic medium or strong basic medium. When the acidity of the medium was at pH < 4, the active sites on the C-dots@cytopore surface were mostly occupied by protons, whereas in the basic medium (pH > 8), precipitation of cadmium caused a significant decline of the adsorption efficiency. Considering that the adsorption efficiency remained virtually constant within a range of pH 4–7, the cadmium standards were prepared in DI water and further treatment was not required. For the processing of real samples, it should be adjusted to pH 6.0 before subject to the preconcentration process.

A series of experiments have been carried out to investigate the cadmium adsorption kinetics on the C-dots@cytopore surface. As can be seen in Fig. S3A, the adsorption was quite a rapid process and the adsorption equilibrium was achieved in a few minutes. Under the experimental conditions, for the cadmium solution of 2μgL−1, 100% adsorption efficiency could be readily achieved within 1min. This was mainly due to the high surface area and the presence of abundant unsaturated binding sites of the carbon dots.

For further elucidation of the adsorption process, pseudo-second order adsorption model was used as expressed in the following. It assumes that the adsorption rate is determined by the square of the number of adsorption sites that are not occupied on the adsorbent surface. The adsorption process is controlled by the chemical adsorption mechanism, which involves the interaction between the adsorbent and the adsorbate or electron transfer. The pseudo-second-order model is represented by Eq. (1) which is one of the linear forms. Fig. S3B showed the adsorption of cadmium for the pseudo-second-order model as a plot of t/q
t against time. The good linear fitting in Fig. S3B indicated that the experimental data well fit the pseudo-second-order kinetics model with a r
2 value of 0.9991, together with the best-fits rate constant of 0.2491gμg−1 h−1. The adsorption rate constant was also corresponding with the dynamic process of rapid reaction. The relevant parameters were given in Table S2, where k
2 (g μg−1 h−1) is the rate constant of pseudo-second-order adsorption, q e (μg g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity, and q
t (μg g−1) is the amount of the solute adsorbed at time t.
(1)
t
q
t
=
1
k
2
q
e
2
+
t
q
e

The adsorption capacity of cadmium by C-dots@cytopore was investigated by adopting the static adsorption equilibrium approach under vigorous stirring for 10min. The cadmium adsorption isotherm was shown in Fig. S4, which well fit the Langmuir adsorption model. A linear plot of C
e /q
e (q
e is the equilibrium adsorbate concentration on C-dots@cytopore, while C
e is the equilibrium cadmium concentration) as the function of C
e results in the maximum adsorption capacity (q
max ) of 2420μgg−1. Our previous experiments have shown that there was almost no cadmium adsorbed by bare cytopore beads. The significant improvement of the adsorption capacity was due to the decoration of carbon dots, which provided much higher effective surface area and more active sites for Cd2+ adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The recovery of cadmium from C-dots@cytopore
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery of retained cadmium from the solid adsorbent was a critical issue for further analysis. Considering that HNO3 was preferential in GFAAS atomization and quantification, nitric acid was selected as the stripping reagent and the influence of eluent volume and concentration on the recovery of cadmium were investigated.

The effect of HNO3 concentration on the elution efficiency of Cd2+ was investigated in the range of 0.01–0.5molL−1 with a fixed eluent volume of 50μL ( Fig. 6A). The results indicated that the increase of HNO3 concentration gave rise to a significant improvement on the stripping efficiency in the range of 0.01–0.1molL−1. Thereafter, a further increase of nitric acid concentration enhanced the blank signal and deteriorated the analytical results. Thus, a 0.1molL−1 HNO3 solution was employed as the suitable eluent.

The effect of eluent volume on the recovery of cadmium was also investigated with a sample volume of 1000μL while varying the eluent volume within 30–150μL (Fig. 6B). It indicated that a too small amount of eluent was not enough to thoroughly recover the retained cadmium, which resulted in a lower elution efficiency. The increase of HNO3 volume could effectively improve the elution efficiency but also accompanied with the subtraction of the enrichment factor. As a compromise between the elution efficiency and the enrichment factor, 50μL of the eluent (0.1molL−1 HNO3) was employed for cadmium recovery in practice.

In sample pretreatment studies, ionic strength is generally a very important parameter in governing the adsorption and desorption of the analyte. In the present study, the effect of ionic strength on adsorption efficiency and the recovery of cadmium were thoroughly investigated, by varying the ionic strength with regulation of NaCl concentration. The existence of Na+ in the solution could not only balance the negative charge on the surface of carbon dots, but also occupy the effective binding sites. As illustrated in Fig. 7, the adsorption and recovery efficiency were maintained at >90% when the NaCl concentration was below 10mgL−1. With the increase of NaCl concentration, obvious interference was observed as reflected in the decline of cadmium adsorption and recovery efficiency. It could be deduced that the maximum tolerance of ionic strength was 10mgL−1.

The coexisting concomitant ionic species might compete with cadmium and disturb the adsorption process. Hence, the potential interfering effects of some commonly encountered foreign species were evaluated. Table 1 illustrated the tolerance levels of some typical metal species when pre-treating 0.2μgL−1 cadmium within a ± 5% error range. It was shown that 50,000-fold K+ and Na+, 10,000-fold Mg2+, 5000-fold for Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, and 1000-fold for Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+ posed no interference. In practice, for the majority of environmental samples, the content of the above foreign ionic species could be generally controlled below these tolerated concentration levels by appropriate dilution. In this case, the present sample pretreatment procedure could be directly adopted for the preconcentration of ultra-trace cadmium from real environmental samples without using any further sample treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and validation of the method
ce:section.ce:para: The characteristic analytical performance data for the preconcentration of trace cadmium with C-dots@cytopore composites as adsorbent was summarized in Table 2. With a sample volume of 1000μL, an enrichment factor of 17.85 was obtained. The procedure provided a detection limit of 1.8ngL−1 along with a precision of 2.6% RSD at 100ngL−1 (n = 9) within a linear range of 30–300ngL−1. For comparison, the analytical performance data of the developed method and other reported methods for extraction and determination of Cd(II) are listed in Table 3. As can be seen, the LOD obtained by this work was lower than that reported in references. Furthermore, the developed method consumes much less sample/reagents compared with other similar methods.

The present procedure was validated by analyzing the cadmium content in a certificated reference material (GBW(E) 080039, simulate water). The analytical results agreed well with the certificated value as detailed in Table 4. The practical application of the proposed procedure was further demonstrated by analyzing the cadmium content in a series of water samples along with spiking recoveries. The obtained results were summarized in Table 4. It was indicated that satisfactory spiking recoveries within the range of 100.0–101.0% were achieved.

A novel selective nano-adsorbent for cadmium, i.e., supported carbon dots composite material (C-dots@cytopore) was prepared. The assembly strategy reported herein opens up a novel avenue for the development of water-soluble carbon nanostructure (carbon dots) served as solid phase extraction adsorbent. The supported carbon dots composites showed superior adsorption property, which could reach the adsorption equilibrium in a very short time of 1min. The fast dynamic adsorption process provided the possibility for rapid separation, enrichment and detection of trace metal elements. The successful establishment of this method expanded the scope of the application of carbon dots on selective solid phase extraction of heavy metals. In view of the rapid and simple synthesis of the composites and the fast adsorption dynamics, the composites can be manufactured in bulk and potentially applied for the rapid removal of heavy metal ions in industrial effluent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21675019, 21375013, 21235001, 21475017), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N141008001, N160302001) and the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201702) are highly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.12.020.
